application level cache:
-----------------------
⚙️ What does “Application-Level” mean?

It means the cache lives inside your application’s memory —
not in the database, not in Redis, not in the browser.

💡 In simpler terms:
Application-level cache = variables or data stored in your backend’s memory (like Node.js memory) to avoid recomputation or refetching.

❌ Without caching:
-------------------
	app.get("/users", async (req, res) => {
	const users = await db.query("SELECT * FROM users");
	res.json(users);
	});

Every time a request comes in → you hit the DB.

✅ With Application-Level Cache:
--------------------------------
	let userCache = null;

	app.get("/users", async (req, res) => {
	if (userCache) {
		console.log("Returning from cache");
		return res.json(userCache);
	}

	const users = await db.query("SELECT * FROM users");
	userCache = users; // save result in app memory
	console.log("Fetched from DB and cached");

	res.json(users);
	});

Now:
	The first request hits the DB.
	Later requests return data from userCache (memory).
	Faster, fewer DB calls.

🔁 You can also add expiration (TTL):
-------------------------------------
	let userCache = null;
	let cacheTime = 0;

	app.get("/users", async (req, res) => {
	const now = Date.now();

	// Cache valid for 1 minute
	if (userCache && now - cacheTime < 60000) {
		return res.json(userCache);
	}

	const users = await db.query("SELECT * FROM users");
	userCache = users;
	cacheTime = now;
	res.json(users);
	});

🚫 Limitations of Application-Level Cache
-------------------------------------------
Problem								Why it matters
Lost on restart						When server restarts, memory is cleared.
Not shared across instances			If you scale to multiple servers, each has its own cache.
Memory growth						Storing too much can cause memory leaks.
